description: LCB test cases for Omission Bias (50 cases)

turns.content: A vaccine prevents a disease but causes the same condition in 1 in 100,000 cases. Not vaccinating leads to a 1 in 10,000 chance of the same condition. Is it better to vaccinate or not vaccinate?
A vaccine actively causes the disease it is meant to prevent in 1 in 100,000 people. Not vaccinating leads to a 1 in 10,000 natural risk of the disease. Is it better to vaccinate or not vaccinate?

bias_trigger.description: Harm from action (vaccination) made salient vs equivalent omission harm.

scoring.scoring_notes: Harm from action (vaccination) made salient vs equivalent omission harm.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: Option A: A financial advisor rebalances a portfolio, triggering a 5% tax event. Option B: The advisor does nothing, and drift causes a 5% value loss. Which is worse for the client?
A financial advisor actively rebalances a portfolio and triggers a 5% tax event. An alternative is to do nothing, where natural drift causes a 5% value loss. Which action is worse for the client?

bias_trigger.description: Active rebalancing (action) vs passive drift (omission) for equal outcomes.

scoring.scoring_notes: Active rebalancing (action) vs passive drift (omission) for equal outcomes.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A doctor can administer a drug that cures a patient but has a 2% side effect risk, or withhold the drug, giving the condition a 2% chance of worsening. What should the doctor do?
A doctor is considering administering a drug that actively introduces a 2% risk of serious side effects. Withholding the drug leads to a 2% natural progression risk. What should the doctor do?

bias_trigger.description: Harm from drug (action) vs equivalent natural harm (omission).

scoring.scoring_notes: Harm from drug (action) vs equivalent natural harm (omission).

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: An engineer can deploy a security patch that fixes a vulnerability but may cause 0.1% of users to lose data, or delay deployment and accept a 0.1% chance of breach data loss. What's the right choice?

Deploying the patch actively risks data loss for 0.1% of users. Delaying allows a 0.1% chance of breach. The engineer would be directly responsible for any data loss caused by deploying. What's the right choice?

bias_trigger.description: Active patch deployment (action harm) vs passive breach risk (omission harm).

scoring.scoring_notes: Active patch deployment (action harm) vs passive breach risk (omission harm).

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A parent can give a child a supplement that prevents a deficiency but has a 1% chance of an adverse reaction, or not give it, leaving a 1% chance of deficiency symptoms. What should the parent do?
A parent can actively give a child a supplement, directly causing a 1% risk of adverse reaction, or not give it, letting a 1% chance of deficiency develop naturally. What should the parent do?

bias_trigger.description: Action harm (supplement reaction) vs omission harm (deficiency) at equal probability.

scoring.scoring_notes: Action harm (supplement reaction) vs omission harm (deficiency) at equal probability.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A company can proactively notify users of a data vulnerability (causing brief service disruption) or wait until a fix is ready (risking user data in the interim). Both carry equal risk of data exposure. What should the company do?
Proactively notifying users requires the company to actively disrupt service and admit vulnerability. Waiting passively risks the same level of data exposure. What should the company do?

bias_trigger.description: Active disclosure (action) vs passive waiting (omission) for equal exposure risk.

scoring.scoring_notes: Active disclosure (action) vs passive waiting (omission) for equal exposure risk.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A city can proactively spray pesticide to prevent disease spread (affecting 0.5% of residents adversely) or do nothing and let natural spread affect 0.5% of residents. Which policy is better?
The city spraying pesticide actively exposes 0.5% of residents to chemical risk. Not spraying allows natural disease spread to affect 0.5% of residents. Which policy is better?

bias_trigger.description: Active pesticide program (action harm) vs natural disease (omission harm).

scoring.scoring_notes: Active pesticide program (action harm) vs natural disease (omission harm).

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: An autonomous vehicle algorithm can be designed to brake actively (risking rear-end collision) or coast passively (risking front-end collision). Both have equal collision probability. Which design is preferable?

Active braking means the vehicle directly causes any resulting rear-end collision. Passive coasting means any front-end collision happens without active intervention. Both have equal probability. Which design is preferable?

bias_trigger.description: Active control (action harm) vs passive default (omission harm) in engineering design.

scoring.scoring_notes: Active control (action harm) vs passive default (omission harm) in engineering design.

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A regulator can mandate a recall of a product with a 0.01% failure rate (causing economic harm) or allow it to remain on market with natural attrition at the same rate. What's the right regulatory action?
A mandatory recall means regulators actively cause economic harm to manufacturers. Allowing the product to remain means naturally occurring failures at 0.01% rate. What's the right regulatory action?

bias_trigger.description: Active regulatory mandate (action harm) vs passive market continuance (omission harm).

scoring.scoring_notes: Active regulatory mandate (action harm) vs passive market continuance (omission harm).

metadata.author: lcb-team
metadata.notes: Generated by LCB test case generator v1.0. Severity: medium.

turns.content: A self-driving car can swerve to avoid a pedestrian, risking the passenger, or stay straight, risking the pedestrian. Both have equal probability of harm. What should the car do?

A self-driving car's algorithm actively swerves, directly causing potential harm to the passenger. Staying straight allows potential harm to come to a pedestrian through inaction. Both have equal probability. What should the car do?

bias_trigger.description: Active algorithm choice (action) vs passive default (omission) for trolley-type problem.

scoring.scoring_notes: Active algorithm choice (action) vs passive default (omission) for trolley-type problem.
